text: These children are test subjects, and this experiment could save your life. So in 1941, there was this huge measles outbreak in the United States. This is a highly contagious virus. It can hang out in the air for up to two hours. Today, we have a vaccine for it, but in the 40s, every kid in this room was vulnerable, so to protect them, researchers ran an experiment with this device that emitted ultraviolet light, and something crazy happened. In rooms with no UV chambers, over half the children got measles. But in these rooms, the number of kids who got sick was less than 15%. What? But wait, isn't UV light dangerous? Doesn't it cause cancer? Well, only sometimes. So UV light that comes from the sun has a wide range of wavelengths that penetrate deep into the skin and damage DNA. So researchers searched the UV spectrum until they found a type of light that's safe for humans, and they landed here on something called far UV. The wavelengths here are too small to penetrate the skin, so they won't cause cancer, but they still wipe out germs. So now we're looking for ways that we can use light as a disinfectant in our everyday lives. So if you believe there should be more optimistic science and tech stories, follow along.

So in 1941, there was this huge measles outbreak in the United States.
This is a highly contagious virus. It can hang out in the air for up to two hours.
Today, we have a vaccine for it, but in the 40s, every kid in this room was vulnerable,
so to protect them, researchers ran an experiment with this device that emitted ultraviolet light,
and something crazy happened. In rooms with no UV chambers, over half the children got measles.
But in these rooms, the number of kids who got sick was less than 15%.
What? But wait, isn't UV light dangerous? Doesn't it cause cancer?
Well, only sometimes. So UV light that comes from the sun has a wide range of wavelengths
that penetrate deep into the skin and damage DNA.
So researchers searched the UV spectrum until they found a type of light that's safe for humans,
and they landed here on something called far UV.
The wavelengths here are too small to penetrate the skin, so they won't cause cancer,
but they still wipe out germs. So now we're looking for ways that we can use
light as a disinfectant in our everyday lives.
So if you believe there should be more optimistic science and tech stories, follow along.
